card.intuition: Maintain a second 'min-tracker' stack in parallel — every time you push, also push the current minimum; every time you pop, the previous minimum is automatically restored.
card.analogy: A sticky note on every document in your stack that says the smallest number seen so far in the pile below it. Remove the top document and its note tells you the new minimum instantly.
card.time_complexity: O(1) for push, pop, top, and getMin
card.space_complexity: O(n) — up to 2n entries across both stacks
when_to_use: Any stack that must support O(1) minimum query in addition to standard push/pop
As a building block for more complex data structures (monotonic stack variants, stack-based DP)
Interview: 'design a data structure with push, pop, and getMin all in O(1)'
gotchas: The min stack must push even when the new value is not a minimum — otherwise pops become desynchronised
Space optimisation: only push to the min stack when the new value is <= current min, but then you must also push on equals to handle duplicates
Alternative single-stack trick: store (val, current_min) pairs — no second stack needed

exercise.prompt: Complete the MinStack. push, pop, top, and getMin must all run in O(1).
hints: When pushing, append min(val, self.min_stack[-1]) to the min stack — this records the smallest value seen up to and including this point
When popping, pop from both stacks to keep them synchronised
exercise.test_code:
ms = MinStack()
ms.push(-2); ms.push(0); ms.push(-3)
assert ms.getMin() == -3, 'Min after [-2,0,-3] is -3'
ms.pop()
assert ms.top()    ==  0, 'Top after pop is 0'
assert ms.getMin() == -2, 'Min after pop is -2'
ms.push(1)
assert ms.getMin() == -2, 'Min unchanged after push(1)'
ms.push(-5)
assert ms.getMin() == -5, 'New min is -5'
ms.pop()
assert ms.getMin() == -2, 'Min restored to -2'
print('PASS')
